ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class; no specific honey-sweetened packaged RTD green tea subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers ready-to-drink green tea; no honey-specific variant code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for packaged honey green tea beverage identified in the published SNOMED CT hierarchy as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard commercially packaged bottle of 500 ml, equivalent to approximately 500 g, is a typical single-serve portion for this ready-to-drink honey green tea.

nutrient_source.title: Honey Green Tea (Packaged) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged honey green tea (cr_id D09020046). All per-100g values reflect the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for packaged honey green tea found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 using sucrose-sweetened beverage analogy (GI ~55–65 for SSBs) adjusted downward for the honey-and-green-tea matrix, which has a modest glycaemic-dampening effect relative to pure sucrose solutions. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (47.55 g per 500 g bottle) / 100 = 24.7. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.09 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; liquid sugar intake is independently associated with elevated blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 9.09 g sugar/100 g classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), and regular SSB consumption is independently associated with elevated blood pressure regardless of its low sodium content of 14 mg/100 g (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.09 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugars are rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should not consume this beverage; its 9.09 g sugar/100 g with zero fibre and an estimated GI of 52 delivers approximately 47.6 g rapidly absorbed carbohydrate per 500 ml bottle, causing significant postprandial hyperglycaemia — choose genuinely unsweetened packaged green tea instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this honey-sweetened packaged tea; habitual SSB intake (≥1 serving/day) is associated with a 20% higher risk of cardiovascular events (Malik et al., Circulation 2010), and this product's 9.09 g sugar/100 g qualifies it as an SSB.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.09 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity relevant to RA; no meaningful anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit is retained in this packaged sweetened form.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; the 9.09 g added sugar/100 g promotes inflammatory cytokine activity, and the dilute packaged formulation provides negligible EGCG anti-inflammatory benefit — choose unsweetened green tea to access any residual polyphenol benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Green Tea (Packaged) is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink (RTD) green tea beverage sweetened with honey, widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia in single-serve plastic or glass bottles typically ranging from 350 ml to 500 ml. The beverage is brewed from green tea extract and blended with honey and sometimes additional sugar to achieve a lightly sweet, mildly astringent flavour profile with a pale golden-green colour. The texture is thin and watery, similar to lightly sweetened water, with no perceptible body. Per 100 g, it provides 30 kcal, 0.04 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 9.51 g carbohydrate (of which 9.09 g is sugar), 0.0 g dietary fibre, and 14 mg sodium. A standard 500 ml bottle delivers approximately 150 kcal and 47.6 g of rapidly absorbed sugar. Despite the marketing association with green tea health benefits, the high sugar content (9.09 g/100 g) classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions. The honey origin of the sugar does not confer meaningful glycaemic advantage over sucrose in a liquid matrix. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its SSB classification, rapid glucose absorption profile, and absence of dietary fibre. Genuinely unsweetened packaged green tea is the recommended alternative for those seeking the polyphenol benefits of green tea without the glycaemic burden.
common_user_queries: Is packaged honey green tea suitable for diabetics?
How much sugar is in bottled honey green tea?
Can I drink honey green tea if I have high blood pressure?
Is RTD green tea with honey good for weight loss?
What is the calorie count of a bottle of honey green tea?
Is honey green tea a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Does honey green tea have anti-inflammatory benefits?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020046) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The food is an exact match in the HPB canonical database. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only) as no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09020046; this is consistent with a water-based tea beverage containing no added fats or oils — confirmed zero by formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same rationale. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; tea infusions and RTD tea beverages contain no measurable dietary fibre in the liquid phase — confirmed zero per HPB. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged RTD honey green tea are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D09020046 as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-sweetened beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available and is recommended. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = 52 × 47.55 / 100 = 24.7. The product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under HPB Singapore and WHO criteria (sugar ≥5 g/100 g in a beverage). Honey as a sweetener does not exempt this product from SSB classification in clinical dietary guidance.
